learning_outcomes.description: Demonstrate an awareness of recent developments in research into effective teaching in inclusive classroom environments and the differentiation of teaching in ways that are responsive to the learning strengths and needs of students across the full range of abilities and that take account of the impact on learning of diverse backgrounds (e.g. linguistic, Indigenous, cultural, religious and socio-economic).

Reflect on legislative requirements, system policies and processes, positive behaviour supports, and teaching strategies that promote student wellbeing and safety, and support participation and learning of all students with a focus on those with disabilities.

Plan challenging learning goals and effectively organise classroom activities for students of diverse backgrounds and across the full range of abilities using age-appropriate curriculum and approaches to differentiated teaching strategies.

Explain approaches for interpreting student data and strategies for working appropriately with others, including parents/carers, external professionals and community groups to action a personal learning plan and modify teaching practices.
Demonstrate clear, fluent, and coherent communication skills consistent with personal, professional and academic conventions.

assessments.assessment_title: Task 1 - Adjusted learning activity development and literature review of a learning need in a discipline area
assessments.task_description:
See task description on Blackboard.

In this task, you will develop a lesson plan to meet the learning needs of a range of students such as those with disability, from diverse backgrounds, including Indigenous learners, and with a range of abilities. The lesson plan will be applicable to your teaching area and based on the Australian Curriculum.

Using the principles of Universal Design for Learning (UDL), you will plan a lesson demonstrating teaching strategies that support student participation (including strategies for Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander students), as well as strategies for assessing what students know or are able to do as a result of the lesson. In addition to the general planning for all, you will plan learning adjustments based on a learning need, indicating appropriate adjustments for teaching and assessing a student with that need.

To plan learning adjustments, you will undertake a review of the available literature to investigate a learning need with respect to your discipline area, where possible, selected from one of the following four options:

1.      Language – e.g., dyslexia, low literacy level

2.      Social / emotional – e.g., Autism Spectrum Disorder, mental health impacts

3.      Learning attainment – e.g., low attainment, intellectual disability, gifted learners, ADHD

4.      Physical and sensory – e.g., vision impairment, sensory sensitivity, paraplegia

 

Format

Use following headings for your submission.

 

Context

A brief overview explaining the context for the lesson such as: teaching area, year level, the specific learning need you have addressed and a discussion of relevant APST standards. This is to provide background for the lesson.


Research background

Include a brief summary of the important research findings for your chosen learning support need.

Analyse the literature with respect to Standards One and Four of the Australian Professional Standards for Teachers, identifying implications for teaching.

A literature review is more than a summary and draws conclusions from research

 

Lesson plan

In a format of your choice, state:

-       Learning goals that provide achievable challenges for all students.

-       Plan teaching and learning activities to achieve the learning goals, including enabling and extending prompts[1]

-       Indicate UDL design features[2] for:

o  Engagement

o  Representation

o  Action and Expression

-       Assessment strategies (formative or summative)

 

Discussion

Provide an explanation of your lesson design decisions, including:

-       an explanation of adjustments designed to meet the learning need you have investigated

-       an explanation of teaching strategies you have chosen and how they are responsive to the learning strengths and needs of students from diverse linguistic, cultural, religious and socioeconomic backgrounds.

 

References

The reference list contains only sources you have cited in the assignment.

Use APA 7th format. Consult the library guides for details of this very specific style.

 

Submission notes

 

In preparation for submission of this task, you should undertake a literature search of the UQ library catalogue, and the listed course outline references. Only include peer-reviewed sources available in the UQ library collection.


Suggestions to assist your literature review

1.      Choose your learning need option.

2.      Read the sections in your text that are relevant.

3.      Write down keywords/phrases.

e.g., if your teaching area is geography and you are interested in challenging/extending learners, you might begin with the following keywords: Geography; Secondary education (if you don’t get much, you could broaden this to school education); gifted; enrichment; intellectual disability, learning difficulty … you can find other keywords once you start to search.

The UQ librarians are happy to assist you with searching for literature on your chosen topic. You may find the library’s help page on search strategies to be helpful. https://web.library.uq.edu.au/research-tools-techniques/uq-library-search

Research articles include a review of relevant literature as part of the background or introductory sections. Your review for this assignment could follow the same style.

It is not a requirement of this task to access literature beyond the UQ library catalogue or readily accessible websites. It is important to judge the quality of the literature and for academic writing, the usual minimum standard is literature that has been peer reviewed. Journals will specify their review processes. Webpages need caution. Ask your librarian or tutor if you are unsure of the quality of the literature you have found.

Referencing your work is an essential aspect of academic writing. The School of Education (and many education journals) specifies the use of APA 7th style for referencing. You can find more information about referencing here: https://guides.library.uq.edu/referencing/apa7

[1] Enabling and extending prompts represent a technique developed to support learners from a range of abilities. See: Sullivan, P., Mousley, J., & Zevenbergen, R. (2006). Teacher actions to maximize mathematics learning opportunities in heterogeneous classrooms. International journal of science and mathematics education, 4, 117-143.

[2] These are the key domains of the current UDL model.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Via Turnitin in the Assessment folder on Blackboard.

assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 1 grade for each 24 hour period from time submission is due will apply for up to 7 days. After 7 days you will receive a mark of 0.

assessments.assessment_title: Task 2 - Case study: Response to scenario
assessments.task_description:
See task description on Blackboard.

In this task, you will build on your understandings of building inclusive classrooms by documenting preparations for a meeting with parents.

Scenario: Two weeks into the new school year, the parents of a student in your year 9 class have asked to meet with you to discuss how you will accommodate their child’s learning needs as specified in the Individual Education Plan[1] (IEP).

In this task, you will document your preparation for the meeting, including the following:

1.      Background

  • A summary of the relevant legislation and policy documents, and a teacher’s legal obligations, related to this scenario. (Choose a jurisdiction where you plan to teach.)
  • A plan of adjustments to be made based on learning, assessment, and behaviour as appropriate to the student’s needs. Justify your plan based on research evidence and subject matter expertise.
  • An indication of the assessment data (formative and summative) you would study prior to the meeting to provide background on the student’s progress.
  • An overview of your investigation of applicable external professionals (e.g., allied health professionals) and community representatives including professional support networks, such as disability associations, and other community groups for extending your personal professional knowledge and practice.
  • A brief summary of relevant research evidence identifying how to effectively engage with families as partners in promoting learning appropriate to the student’s stage of learning.


2.      A plan for the meeting

  • Location, timing, seating arrangement, refreshments etc.
  • Introductions – opportunity for parents to indicate what they wish to gain from the meeting.
  • How you will work with parents/carers over the year, including considerations of sensitivity, confidentiality, seeking their input and advice, and how you intend to collaborate with them throughout the year. You may wish to consider engagement strategies, such as, the use of two-way positive communication, collaborative planning and problem-solving, and how you will use light-touch learning updates (e.g. brief, regular updates), among other approaches.
  • Explanation of learning adjustments applicable to your subject area in response to the student’s IEP.
  • Explanation of reasonable adjustments planned for assessment, in response to the IEP
  • Explanation of your plan for the student’s wellbeing and safety, including positive behaviour support processes (Tier 1, 2 or 3, as applicable).


3.      Follow up

·        Recording of the conversation.

·        Further meetings.

Reporting to others.

[1] Individual Education Plan is internationally recognised terminology. Local jurisdictions may use different terminology e.g. personalised learning plans.

assessments.task_description: A one-hour examination will use multiple choice and short answer responses to assess understanding of the course content provided in the learning modules on the course learning management system.

learning_activities.topic:
Embracing inclusive practice Week 1. Lecture and Tutorial 1 set the context for this course. They introduce the concept of inclusion and begin the work of developing an evidence base for practice. Introduction to UDL.

This first week's activities set the context for this course. It introduces students to the concept of inclusion and begins the work of developing an evidence base for practice. Introduction to UDL.

Embracing inclusive practice: Lecture and Tutorial 1 set the context for this course. They introduce the concept of inclusion and begin the work of developing an evidence base for practice. Introduction to UDL.

The first nine weeks of the course teach the core content of focus area: Diverse learning needs including students with disability. The emphasis is on the evidence base, ensuring you are equipped to build your own personal understanding of the importance of inclusive education. For examples, in this first lecture on embracing inclusive practice, you are introduced to the research evidence supporting highly effective inclusive practices, including systematic reviews of evidence for inclusive practice and how these practices benefit all students, including those with additional needs or from disadvantaged backgrounds (CC 4.4.1, 4.4.2 – taught). Learning outcomes: L01, L02


Learning outcomes: L01, L02

learning_activities.topic:
Understanding the legislative context - Evidence for Inclusive Education Week 2

Understanding the legislative context - Evidence for Inclusive Education (CC 4.4.2 – taught).

In this week’s tutorial, you are taught how to use search techniques to select peer-reviewed research literature identifying research evidence for inclusive practice. You then practise these skills by identifying relevant research literature and discussing the findings in groups. This leads to Task 1 where your understanding of the evidence is assessed and to Task 2 where practical strategies are planned into a lesson to meet specific learning support needs (CC 4.4.1, 4.4.3 – practised). Learning outcomes: L01, L02


Learning outcomes: L01, L02

Understanding the legislative context - The right to quality inclusive education Week 3. This module focuses on the legislation and policy context that underpins students' rights to quality education

This module focuses on the legislation and policy context that underpins students' rights to quality education.

Understanding the legislative context - The right to quality inclusive education: This module focuses on the legislation and policy context that underpins students' rights to quality education (CC 4.4.2 – taught).

This leads to Assessment Task 3 where this knowledge is assessed (CC 4.4.2 – practised). Learning outcomes: L01

learning_activities.topic:
Planning for Learning - Universal Design for Learning; Using technology in the classroom Week 5. Advances in ICT have had a dramatic effect on accessibility of learning. Advances in ICT have had a dramatic effect on accessibility of learning. Many features are now built into devices.

Planning for Learning - Universal Design for Learning; Using technology in the classroom: Advances in ICT have had a dramatic effect on accessibility of learning. Many features are now built into devices. Bring along your favourite piece of learning technology to demonstrate.

In this lecture, you are taught the basis of planning for the majority of learning support needs through the UDL approach. You are introduced to the evidence base for this UNESCO recognised approach to planning. You are taught strategies that make use of technology to support learners with sensory impairments, including vision and hearing (CC 4.4.3 – taught).

You share the accessibility features of a technological device (e.g., a smart phone) in presentations to your tutorial group. You also investigate the AAC (Augmentative and Alternative Communication) devices available through the UQ library and from resources centres (e.g., Vision Australia) (CC 4.4.3 – practised). Learning outcomes: L03, L05


Learning outcomes: L03, L05

learning_activities.topic:
Planning for Learning - Year level adjusted curriculum Week 6. The Australian Curriculum is a year-level curriculum. The implications for planning, particularly with respect to gifted learners and learners with intellectual disabilities will be explored.

The Australian Curriculum is a year-level curriculum. The implications for planning, particularly with respect to gifted learners and learners with intellectual disabilities will be explored.

Planning for Learning - Year level adjusted curriculum: The Australian Curriculum is a year-level curriculum. The implications for planning, particularly with respect to gifted learners and learners with intellectual disabilities will be explored.

In this lecture, you are taught how to support learners at various levels of attainment, based on Learning Year Level Adjusted Curriculum, with supporting research (CC 4.4.3 – taught).

In this tutorial, you collaborate to plan options for lesson features to support diverse learning needs using the UDL framework. In addition, you practise designing learning adjustments for students with individual education plans requiring supports beyond what is provided through UDL (CC 4.4.3 – practised). Learning outcomes: L03

Learning outcomes: L03

learning_activities.topic:
Planning for Learning - Assessment Week 7. Assessment: Assessment has a range of purposes, sometimes referred to as: Assessment for learning - so teachers and their students can make learning progress; Assessment of learning - finding out what students know and can do as a result of teaching; Assessment as learning - undertaking tasks can lead to learning. Designing and interpreting assessment in the context of inclusive practice is the focus of this module.

Planning for Learning – Assessment: Assessment has a range of purposes, sometimes referred to as: Assessment for learning - so teachers and their students can make learning progress; Assessment of learning - finding out what students know and can do as a result of teaching; Assessment as learning - undertaking tasks can lead to learning. Designing and interpreting assessment in the context of inclusive practice is the focus of this module.

Assessment strategies that promote learning are taught in this lecture. Adjustments to assessment are a legal requirement for students with disabilities and you are taught how to plan these adjustments. This leads to Task 2 where formative assessment is required to be designed into the lesson plan. To support this lecture, professional learning resources from AITSL, ACARA and Assessment Guides are used, in addition to textbook references (CC 4.4.3 – taught). Learning outcomes: L03

Learning outcomes: L03

learning_activities.topic:
Planning for Learning - Language and Literacy support Week 8. Language support is the work of all teachers. Some students will have specific language difficulties and disabilities. Some will have learning strengths. Guest presenter from Speech Therapy will discuss language disorders and how speech language pathologists work with teachers.

Planning for Learning - Language and Literacy support: Language support is the work of all teachers. Some students will have specific language difficulties and disabilities. Some will have learning strengths. Guest presenter from Speech Therapy will discuss language disorders and how speech language pathologists work with teachers.

In this lecture, you learn evidence-based approaches for supporting students with language disorders and specific learning difficulties. The lecture is given by a speech pathology academic from UQ’s School of Health and Rehabilitation Science, who explains how speech language pathologists work with teachers and evidence-based strategies to support secondary teachers’ work. Current research in the speech pathology field is referenced by the guest lecturer (CC 4.4.3 – taught).

This tutorial aligns with the lecture on developmental language disorder and other language learning difficulties. You practise strategies such as making adjustments to the language level of texts to support learners in secondary classrooms (CC 4.4.3 – practised). Learning outcomes: L03, L05

Learning outcomes: L03, L05

learning_activities.topic:
Planning for Learning - Positive Behaviour Support Week 9. Establishing and maintaining a classroom for learning relies on supporting students to exhibit productive learning behaviours. Behaviour is communication so responding to the need being expressed is more effective than responding to the behaviour itself. This module focuses on an approach known as 'Positive Behaviour Supports'.

Planning for Learning - Positive Behaviour Support: Establishing and maintaining a classroom for learning relies on supporting students to exhibit productive learning behaviours. Behaviour is communication so responding to the need being expressed is more effective than responding to the behaviour itself. This module focuses on an approach known as 'Positive Behaviour Supports'.

In the lecture, you learn about the psychological basis to Positive Behaviour Support strategies and explore how to implement these practices in their classrooms. These assist teachers to establish and maintain classroom environments for learning. You learn to look to the motivation behind behaviours to support students with diverse needs (CC 4.4.3 – taught).

In the tutorial, you discuss antecedents and consequences of common classroom behaviours to identify motivations behind the observed behaviours. You then consider responses teachers might make to the situation to assist learners to develop positive behaviours for learning (CC 4.4.3 – practised). Learning outcomes: L03, L05

Learning outcomes: L03, L05

learning_activities.topic:
Building a Community of Practice - Community resources Week 13.

A panel of experts comprising a head of learning support, a disability expert (relevant to the disability focus of the IEP) and a parent discuss the context of meeting with a parent. You are encouraged to ask any questions you may have. This two-hour session allows you to understand the context from multiple perspectives. The disability expert also teaches evidence informed strategies. This builds towards Assessment Task 2 where you prepare a plan for the meeting. (CC 4.3.1, 4.3.2, 4.4.3 – taught).

Reflecting on the research evidence covered in the lecture and weekly reading, in this week’s tutorial, you practice engaging with parents in a meeting. This builds towards your response for Assessment Task 3 where you prepare a plan for the meeting. This practice involves taking part in role plays where you take turns to adopt the roles of parent, teacher or student (CC 4.3.1, 4.3.2 – practised). You learn to locate resources relevant to the education of learners with specific disabilities and judge source quality (CC 4.4.3 – practised). Learning outcomes: L04, L05

Learning outcomes: L04, L05

learning_activities.topic:
Week 12 - working with others

Building a Community of Practice - Working with others: Teachers work in teams, often as the leader. When a student with an individual learning plan is in the class, many people may be involved in the community of practice. These could be teacher aides, specialist teachers, therapists, parents and learning support teams. This module will explore ways of building positive relationships, including running meetings.

This lecture focuses on working with parents/carers, specialist teachers, other professionals (such as speech pathologists) and teacher aides. The importance of building respectful relationships and holding a non-judgemental positive regard for families is stressed. Research evidence for engaging families to support student learning is covered here as the lecture explores the importance of collaboration and communication in promoting student learning and wellbeing. Important frameworks are outlined to explain effective school-family partnerships (CC 4.3.1, 4.3.2 – taught).

In this tutorial, you practice strategies for promoting behaviours that support learning in a classroom. You practise approaches for working with families when issues arise. This is developed further in later tutorials (CC 4.3.2 – practised). In addition, the tutorial involves practising strategies for working with teams in classrooms and with building relationships with families. You practise how to collaborate with others, including families, and practise implementing requirements of an individual learning plan as the requirements affect their subject area. This builds to Assessment Task 3 (CC 4.3.2 – practised). Learning outcomes: L04
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 12 - working with others Building a Community of Practice - Working with others: Teachers work in teams, often as the leader. When a student with an individual learning plan is in the class, many people may be involved in the community of practice. These could be teacher aides, specialist teachers, therapists, parents and learning support teams. This module will explore ways of building positive relationships, including running meetings. This lecture focuses on working with parents/carers, specialist teachers, other professionals (such as speech pathologists) and teacher aides. The importance of building respectful relationships and holding a non-judgemental positive regard for families is stressed. Research evidence for engaging families to support student learning is covered here as the lecture explores the importance of collaboration and communication in promoting student learning and wellbeing. Important frameworks are outlined to explain effective school-family partnerships (CC 4.3.1, 4.3.2 – taught). In this tutorial, you practice strategies for promoting behaviours that support learning in a classroom. You practise approaches for working with families when issues arise. This is developed further in later tutorials (CC 4.3.2 – practised). In addition, the tutorial involves practising strategies for working with teams in classrooms and with building relationships with families. You practise how to collaborate with others, including families, and practise implementing requirements of an individual learning plan as the requirements affect their subject area. This builds to Assessment Task 3 (CC 4.3.2 – practised).
